food_name: Roti Prata, Plain (single piece, with dhal curry — Singapore mamak / Indian Muslim style)
region_of_origin: Singapore / Malaysia — brought by South Indian and Tamil Muslim immigrants; adapted into the lighter, crispier Singapore prata style distinct from the Malaysian roti canai
food_subgroup: Unleavened wheat flour flatbread, kneaded with ghee or margarine until pliable, stretched and spun thin, layered, and cooked on a tawa griddle; served with dhal curry

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has paratha as a category. Singapore roti prata is a specific style of layered wheat flatbread — distinct from the unlayered Indian paratha. No exact FoodOn term exists for Singapore-style roti prata; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Singapore roti prata — layered wheat flatbread with ghee, cooked on tawa (plain variety, served with dhal curry)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EKF is the closest available code for unleavened flatbread; no Singapore-specific roti prata code exists in the standard FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for roti prata or Singapore-style layered flatbread as of 2026.

preparation_method: pan-fried on tawa griddle with ghee

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one piece of plain roti prata (approximately 90g) accompanied by 50g of dhal curry, totalling approximately 140g.

nutrient_source.title: Roti Prata (Plain) — HPB Singapore (Foodo) / ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for roti prata (plain) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables for dhal curry component. Values represent the composite dish (prata + dhal) per 100g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — University of Sydney GI Database; Roti Prata (plain, Singapore-style)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of ~82 (range 80–84) for plain roti prata sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (82 × 39.5g carbohydrate in 140g serving) / 100 ≈ 32.4. High GI driven by refined wheat flour base and ghee-frying method which does not significantly lower GI.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 268mg/100g; a single-piece serving (140g) delivers approximately 375mg sodium — moderate contribution toward the 2,000mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension may consume one piece of plain prata occasionally, but should avoid adding extra salt, limit high-sodium curry accompaniments, and monitor total daily sodium intake from other hawker dishes consumed in the same day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high GI (~82) and high glycemic load per serving (GL ~32.4); refined wheat flour base with ghee produces rapid post-prandial glucose spike comparable to glucose itself.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should strongly limit plain roti prata: if consuming, restrict to one piece maximum, eat the dhal first to prime gut response with fibre and protein, pair with a protein dish, and reduce frequency to no more than once or twice per week — the common misconception that 'prata with dhal is balanced' is clinically dangerous and must be explicitly addressed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (7.5g/100g) from ghee and trans fat (0.1g/100g); a single serving contributes approximately 10.5g saturated fat, exceeding the recommended single-meal saturated fat limit for CVD patients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit plain roti prata due to its high saturated fat content from ghee; if consumed, restrict to one piece infrequently, avoid egg or cheese prata variants, and balance the meal with fibre-rich dhal and vegetable curry to partially offset the lipid impact.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Very high GI drives post-prandial glucose spikes promoting systemic inflammation; high saturated fat from ghee compounds the pro-inflammatory effect.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit prata consumption; if eating prata, pair with anti-inflammatory dhal (folate, protein) and a vegetable curry, as the dhal is the most anti-inflammatory component of the prata meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roti Prata (known as Roti Canai in Malaysia) is Singapore's most universally beloved breakfast bread — eaten across all ethnic communities from dawn at mamak (Indian Muslim) stalls that operate around the clock. The technique of making prata is a mesmerising performance: the dough, enriched with ghee and kneaded until silky, is tossed and spun in the air with expert hands, stretching into an almost translucent sheet before being folded into layers and slapped onto the hot tawa (iron griddle). Ghee is poured around it, and it cooks into a crisp, golden-brown disc — shatteringly crunchy at the edges with soft, fluffy, layered interior sections that tear away in satisfying sheets. It is eaten by tearing pieces and dipping into the accompanying dhal curry — a yellow, mildly spiced split lentil soup that provides a subtle earthy counterpoint. Nutritionally, plain roti prata is energy-dense at 310 kcal/100g, with significant fat (14.5g, predominantly saturated at 7.5g from ghee), moderate protein (7.5g), and high carbohydrate (40g). CRITICAL CLINICAL NOTE: Despite the dhal accompaniment creating an impression of a 'balanced meal', the prata itself has a very high GI (~82) — comparable to glucose in glycaemic impact per gram of carbohydrate. The glycemic load per standard serving (1 piece + dhal, 140g) is approximately 32.4, which is extremely high. A patient eating 2 plain prata for breakfast has consumed a glycaemic load equivalent to drinking a large sugary soft drink. The dhal's low-GI contribution is clinically insufficient to buffer the prata's glycaemic response at typical serving ratios. This dish is a daily staple across Singapore's hawker centres, kopitiams, and 24-hour mamak stalls, and is a key food to address in dietary counselling for diabetes and cardiovascular disease management in Singapore.
common_user_queries: how many calories in one prata
is roti prata good for diabetics

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary regional source for Singapore dishes) and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables. GI value of ~82 from University of Sydney GI Database with direct match for plain roti prata. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from approved sources with confidence 1.0 per input metadata.
notes: vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plain wheat flour flatbread with ghee and lentil dhal contains negligible vitamin C; confirmed per HPB Singapore Foodo and ICMR-NIN). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — this is a vegetarian dish with no animal flesh; trace amounts from ghee are below detection threshold per ICMR-NIN). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no significant vitamin D source in plain prata or dhal; confirmed per HPB Singapore Foodo). monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Singapore-style roti prata composite dish (prata + dhal) not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN databases as of 2026. water_g set to -1.0: moisture content for the composite dish (prata + dhal) not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. Multiple is_default:true portions retained from source data reflecting different common serving configurations (single piece with dhal = 140g; two pieces with dhal = 320g; restaurant/GrabFood serving = 350g). GI of 82 is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database range of 80–84 for plain roti prata. The 'prata with dhal is balanced' misconception is a clinically important point for Singapore dietary counselling.
